PR DESCRIPTION:
### Motivation

In some cases cursor position info can be too large to serialize as a single entry, e.g. in case of too many deleted ranges. Also the serialization can be too slow.

cherry-picks of changes by @eolivelli @nicoloboschi and I.

### Modifications

Cursor PositionInfo serialization is reworked to produce less garbage/serialize faster; serialized data can be compressed.
In case the serialized data too large it is chunked and saved as a sequence of entries.

### Verifying this change

- [ ] Make sure that the change passes the CI checks.

This change added tests.

### Does this pull request potentially affect one of the following parts:

NO

*If the box was checked, please highlight the changes*

- [ ] Dependencies (add or upgrade a dependency)
- [ ] The public API
- [ ] The schema
- [ ] The default values of configurations
- [ ] The threading model
- [ ] The binary protocol
- [ ] The REST endpoints
- [ ] The admin CLI options
- [ ] The metrics
- [ ] Anything that affects deployment

### Documentation

<!-- DO NOT REMOVE THIS SECTION. CHECK THE PROPER BOX ONLY. -->

- [ ] `doc` <!-- Your PR contains doc changes. -->
- [ ] `doc-required` <!-- Your PR changes impact docs and you will update later -->
- [x] `doc-not-needed` <!-- Your PR changes do not impact docs -->
- [ ] `doc-complete` <!-- Docs have been already added -->

### Matching PR in forked repository

PR in forked repository: https://github.com/dlg99/pulsar/pull/

-# If enabled, the maximum "acknowledgment holes" will not be limited and "acknowledgment holes" are stored in
-# multiple entries.
+# If enabled, the maximum "acknowledgment holes" (as defined by managedLedgerMaxUnackedRangesToPersist)

+persistentUnackedRangesMaxEntrySize=1048576

+persistentUnackedRangesWithMultipleEntriesEnabled=false
+

SUMMARY:
I added a few review comments.

POINTS:
- [managed-ledger/src/main/java/org/apache/bookkeeper/mledger/impl/ManagedCursorImpl.java:688] instead of adjusting the writerIndex manually, `buffer.addComponent(true, part)` could be a way to achieve this.
- [managed-ledger/src/main/java/org/apache/bookkeeper/mledger/impl/ManagedCursorImpl.java:3509] use `.addComponent(true, szBuf).addComponent(true, encode)` so that there isn't a need to set the writerIndex manually.
- [managed-ledger/src/main/java/org/apache/bookkeeper/mledger/impl/MetaStoreImpl.java:466] When using LightProto, the buffer shouldn't be released until the parsed instance is no longer used. This is due to the implementation detail that String properties will be lazily resolved from the input buffer.
It's possible to resolve this by making a copy with `ManagedLedgerInfo.copyFrom(ManagedLedgerInfo.parseFrom(decode.nioBuffer())` unless there's a solution in place to ensure that buffers aren't released as long as the instance is used.
- [managed-ledger/src/main/java/org/apache/bookkeeper/mledger/impl/MetaStoreImpl.java:493] Please see the previous comment about LightProto and buffer lifecycle.

It seems to me that most changes in the `JCacheRegionFactory` were done for the sake of ensuring tests can continue functioning as they are. I would prefer if we rather change tests to match the "real world" usage by e.g. providing the pre-built `CacheManager` via the `org.hibernate.cache.jcache.ConfigSettings#CACHE_MANAGER` property.

POINTS:
- [vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/file/impl/FileSystemImpl.java:30] try to keep the original style which is not using * imports: this will make your changes to just be the one re the copy
- [vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/file/impl/FileSystemImpl.java:60] same here, no need to change this since unrelated to the copy

- [vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/file/impl/FileSystemImpl.java:690] you can reintroduce this one  since is not needed
- [vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/file/impl/FileSystemImpl.java:856] @vietj : this is ok? Or you prefer to add a new constructor to `BufferImpl' which still uses the existing Vertx heap types eg VertxUnsafeHeapByteBuf/VertxHeapByteBuf ?
The reason I'm asking is:
- `Unpooled::wrappedBuffer` still have Netty reference count checks under the hood, implemented - which have a cost
- IIRC Vertx itself contains checks to make sure the allocator of a vertx buffer is "known" to vertx e.g. https://github.com/eclipse-vertx/vert.x/blob/ff78924cff931f275238bac3dfc64bacb27991d1/vertx-core/src/main/java/io/vertx/core/net/impl/VertxHandler.java#L48-L67

One pain point is that AFAIK Netty's heap buffers (one of the two variant) doesn't expose in public to directly set the wrapped buffer - and that's why Netty itself is using only non-Unsafe variants in `Unpooled::wrappedBuffer(byte[])`: if we want to do this right in Vertx we could leverage a single implementation (i.e.non-unsafe) as well or modify Netty to expose it for Unsafe variants (I can do it if needed)

SUMMARY:
Overall I think this is a good start.  I have a few questions and suggestions to get started with in this PR and then once those are addressed I want to talk about the ordering of the steps themselves.

POINTS:
- [core/builder/src/main/java/io/quarkus/builder/Execution.java:72] If this value must be a `SortedSet`, then its type should be `SortedSet` rather than having all these dynamic checks around.  All this stuff is package-private I believe.
- [core/builder/src/main/java/io/quarkus/builder/Execution.java:51] Rather than have maps associating things with `StepInfo`, why not just put fields right on the `StepInfo`?
- [core/builder/src/main/java/io/quarkus/builder/BuildChainBuilder.java:69] I'm a bit concerned about using these string values for ordering purposes since they're pretty arbitrary.  I wonder if you considered any other approaches to determining an ordinal value for each step?
- [core/builder/src/main/java/io/quarkus/builder/Execution.java:77] This is another place where it seems like a field on `StepInfo` would be simpler and faster.
- [core/builder/src/main/java/io/quarkus/builder/Execution.java:79] One more thing that could be a field on `StepInfo` instead.
- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/ExtensionLoader.java:874] I'm a bit concerned about this because IIRC until now these names were used *only* for diagnostic (human readable) messages.  Why does it need to be a hash?
- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/builditem/StaticBytecodeRecorderBuildItem.java:12] Adding an ordinal here shouldn't be necessary because the steps were already topologically sorted, therefore the build items produced will also be sorted by the same topology.
- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/builditem/SystemPropertyBuildItem.java:11] These also should not need to be comparable.
- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/logging/LogCleanupFilterBuildItem.java:15] This also should not need to be comparable.

SUMMARY:
In general, the removal of the curved tab support is fine. I see some minor issues (wrong deprecation notices) and have some minor proposals (further cleaning up) in that regard.

However, the PR also removes support for colors/colors gradients in tab header, which does is not part of the intent or at least is unrelated to this PR. That must not be done as a side effect of this PR but, if at all, explicitly in a separate one.

They remove the support for gradients in tab header, which is not the intent of this PR. This is only about removing curved tab support. So all those changes should be removed from this PR. In case we want to remove gradients as well, we should discuss that separately.

SUMMARY:
I am a bit confused by this change, as it introduces a mixture of event processing and direct method calls for DPI change processing. I expected the following order of changes according to what we have discussed:
1. Replace `CommonWidgetsDPIChangeHandler`: use listeners instead
2. Replace `DPIZoomChangeRegistry`: make direct calls of `handleDPIChange` for the native controls instead
3. Replace direct `handleDPIChange` calls: use listeners instead
4. Make the listener calls asynchronous (may be combined with the previous step)

This change seems to be a mixture of the first three. Can we please split that up and make the changes in a more incremental, comprehensive way?

SUMMARY:
I like the general idea of this, but I would have a few general questions in addition to my review comments:

* Are we measuring the number of log messages per level, in an absolute sense?  Or are we measuring the number of log messages that actually appear in the console log/file log/some other specific destination?  
* If the former, then are we measuring only log messages that pass the log level checks of each category?
* What if the log message passes the category level checks but is filtered out by all handlers?  Should it get counted then?

POINTS:
- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/logging/LogMetricsHandler.java:13] These definitely ought to be `final`.
- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/logging/LogBuildTimeConfig.java:14] Why make this optional when the memory usage is negligible and the CPU overhead would be only a small fraction of the cost of logging the message to any handler?  And why make it a build-time property?

IMO it would be simpler and more expedient to leave it on all the time.
- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/logging/LogMetricsHandlerRecorder.java:28] This should be `final`.
- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/logging/LogMetricsHandlerRecorder.java:33] This should be `final`.
- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/logging/LogMetricsHandlerRecorder.java:28] In addition, I would map as many non-standard levels as possible to these counters, for example:

```java
    logCounters.put(java.util.logging.Level.WARNING, logCounters.get(Level.WARN));
```

This will increase the accuracy of the counts.  Note that level equivalency should be determined by the level's integer value.

ForbidCertainImportsCheck.forbiddenImportsRegexp = Regexp for matching forbidden imports ForbidCertainImportsCheck.forbiddenImportsExcludesRegexp = Regexp for excluding imports from checking +ForbidCertainMethodCheck.name = Forbid Certain Method +ForbidCertainMethodCheck.desc = Forbids certain method usage.

You can configure this check using following parameters:
  1. Method name regexp;
  2. Argument count range;
This check reads method & constructor names without class/object name, so, please, do NOT include class/variable name in your regular expressions when configuring.

Real-life example of usage: forbid to use all "exit" method, forbid use of assertTrue/assertFalse with 1 parameter. For doing that, you should use the following check parameters:

  • Method name regexp = "assert(True|False)"
  • Argument count range = "1"


    • . Argument count can be bounded range(e.g.: 2-4) or unbounded range(e.g.: -5, 6-). Unbounded range can be unbounded only on one side. Multiple ranges must be comma separated. Author: Raghav Kumar Gautam +ForbidCertainMethodCheck.methodName = Regex to match name of the forbidden method. When blank or unspecified, all the methods will be allowed. +ForbidCertainMethodCheck.argumentCount = Number or range to match number of arguments the forbidden method takes. - [sevntu-checks/src/main/java/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/ForbidCertainMethodCheck.java:176] please move `log` method out to visitToken to make logic clear to read.
as you do remove, please rename method to `isForbiddenMethod`.
- [sevntu-checks/src/main/java/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/ForbidCertainMethodCheck.java:94] limits or range are by Integer type, so please remove usage of `null` and use Integer.MAX.
IntRange should fail if null is passed to it.
- [sevntu-checks/src/main/java/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/ForbidCertainMethodCheck.java:262] you should not have null in code, you can use 0 or MAX .
